reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The decision-support table comparing custom resources vs. deployment scripts vs. raw CloudFormation vs. CDK constructs is the standout feature — exactly what a new user needs to decide whether to reach for this tool.

The Lambda handler section is unusually thorough: the event field table, response format JSON, and the four handler rules (always respond, stable PhysicalResourceId, graceful Delete, 4096-byte Data limit) are all source-grounded and directly actionable.
The Warning callout about the ResponseURL requirement is well-placed and addresses the single most common mistake new users make with CloudFormation custom resources.
Both Lambda packaging modes (stacktape-lambda-buildpack and custom-artifact) are covered with working code examples and clear when-to-use guidance — good union-type coverage.

The FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, comparison) and the 10-pair format is met. Individual answers are concise and self-contained enough to be extracted as snippets.

The connectTo and iamRoleStatements sections both have working examples and the environment variable naming pattern (STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_NAME, STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_ARN) correctly matches the example resource name 'configBucket'.
problems: CRITICAL: The page explains the two-part model (definition + instance) in the 'How it works' section but never provides a Stacktape config code example showing a CustomResourceInstance. The 'Instances' section has a property table and an Info callout but NO <CodeBlock intellisense> example. A reader who copies the basic example will have a definition with no instances — CloudFormation will create the Lambda function but never invoke it. The page is unusable without this example.

The 'Basic example' only shows the CustomResource definition config. It never shows the companion instance config that references the definition with definitionName and resourceProperties. The reader cannot deploy a working custom resource from this page alone.

The <FlowDiagram> component is used in 'How it works' but is not mentioned in the style guide's component palette, is not present in any referenced source files, and is not listed in the system MDX component inventory. If it does not exist in the docs framework, this section will render broken.

The 'How it works' section partially redundantly restates the conceptual two-part model that is also explained in 'When to use' and the opening paragraph. The FlowDiagram adds little factual content not already covered in prose.
mandatoryFixes: Add a <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}> example to the 'Instances' section showing how to create a CustomResourceInstance (with definitionName referencing the definition name and a resourceProperties key-value pair). This is the second required resource type and without a config example the page is practically incomplete — a reader cannot deploy custom resources from this page.

Update the 'Basic example' (or add a 'Complete example' subsection) to show BOTH the CustomResource definition AND the companion instance in a single defineConfig block. The definition alone does nothing — CloudFormation needs an instance to invoke the backing Lambda. Even a minimal instance with definitionName and a single resourceProperties entry would close this gap.

Verify that <FlowDiagram> exists in the docs component palette. If it does not, replace the component with a numbered list or a prose walkthrough of the same four steps. Leaving an unknown component on the page will break the rendered output.
optionalImprovements: The 'How it works' section could be trimmed or merged with the intro paragraph. The FlowDiagram (if it exists) already restates the prose. If the component is removed, the remaining prose is thin — consider absorbing the two-part model explanation into the basic example's lead-in instead.

The handler example uses 'any' type annotations throughout. Providing a minimal CloudFormation custom resource event type (or linking to @types/aws-lambda) would help TypeScript users without adding significant length.

The 'Runtime' section is a single paragraph. Consider folding it into the 'Packaging' section as a short note under each packaging variant, since runtime auto-detection is packaging-mode-dependent and the context is already established there.

The comparison table in 'When NOT to use' uses 'Yes (Lambda)' and 'Yes (local or bastion)' in the 'Runs code during deploy' row — the column header 'Deployment script' running locally is not technically 'during deploy' in the CloudFormation sense. Clarify the wording to avoid confusion about execution timing.

FAQ answer for 'How long can a custom resource Lambda run?' states the default is 10 seconds — this is correctly sourced from the type definition JSDoc. Consider adding a concrete example of when to increase it (e.g., 'DNS propagation checks may need 60-120 seconds') to make the guidance actionable.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph (45 words) is self-contained, entity-named, and directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — suitable for verbatim extraction.
Comparison table in 'When NOT to use' covers all four alternatives (custom resource, deployment script, raw CloudFormation, CDK construct) with four differentiating dimensions — high citation value.
Lambda handler code block is realistic and copy-paste complete: shows the full CloudFormation response protocol (RequestType switch, try/catch, fetch to ResponseURL) with no placeholder ellipses.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo, instances, return values), AWS use-case (CloudFormation lifecycle, rollback, pricing), and comparison (vs deployment script, vs CDK constructs).
Both Lambda packaging variants (stacktape-lambda-buildpack, custom-artifact) are covered with working code examples and per-variant when-to-use guidance — union-type coverage discipline met.

Decision support for connectTo and iamRoleStatements is strong: separate examples, concrete env-var names (STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_NAME, STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_ARN), and an explicit explanation of when to use each.
The Warning callout about mandatory ResponseURL response is accurately placed and prevents the most common custom resource authoring mistake.
Handler event fields and response format are presented as tables with concrete column names — highly extractable by AI engines.
The two-part model explanation (definition vs. instance) in 'How it works' is conceptually clear and uses named entities throughout.
problems: CRITICAL: No CustomResourceInstance code example anywhere on the page. The two-part model is explained in prose and a property table, but neither the basic example nor the Instances section shows a complete Stacktape config with both a definition and a corresponding instance (definitionName + resourceProperties). A reader cannot deploy a working custom resource without knowing how to declare the instance.

The 4,096 bytes maximum for the Data response payload is stated twice (handler rules prose + FAQ answer) but does not appear in any provided source file. This is an AWS constraint, not a Stacktape-specific claim, but stating it as a hard rule in the handler rules section without caveat carries source-grounding risk.
The 'Basic example' section opens with 'This example defines a custom resource...' — 'This' has no prior sentence antecedent in the section, breaking AEO self-containment for the first paragraph.
The 'Instances' section has no code example showing how to declare a CustomResourceInstance in defineConfig, leaving readers without a runnable reference for the second half of the two-part model.
mandatoryFixes: Add a CustomResourceInstance code example — either extend the basic example CodeBlock to include both the definition (CustomResource) and a matching instance (with definitionName: 'webhookProvider' and concrete resourceProperties), or add a dedicated code block in the Instances section. Without this, the page describes a feature that readers cannot implement from the docs alone.

Fix the custom artifact handler format in the Custom Artifact packaging example: change handler: 'index.handler' (dot notation) to match the source-documented colon format, e.g., handler: 'index.js:handler', consistent with the canonical example 'my-lambda/index.js:default' in CustomArtifactLambdaPackagingProps.
optionalImprovements: Rewrite the Basic example section opening from 'This example defines a custom resource that...' to 'The example below defines a Stacktape custom resource that...' to satisfy AEO self-containment when extracted as a snippet.

Add a caveat to the 4,096-byte Data payload claim (e.g., 'per AWS CloudFormation limits') so it reads as an AWS constraint rather than a Stacktape-sourced fact, and avoid repeating it verbatim in both the handler rules prose and the FAQ.

The 'Instances' section is the only configuration-facing section with no code example. Even a minimal CodeBlock showing { definitionName: 'webhookProvider', resourceProperties: { callbackUrl: 'https://my-app.com/hooks' } } would make the section actionable.

The timeout: 60 value in both packaging sub-examples is never explained (why 60, not 30 or 900). Add one sentence per example acknowledging that 60 seconds is an example value appropriate for slow external APIs and that the max is 900 seconds.

The FlowDiagram component (used in 'How it works') is not in the standard MDX component palette documented for Stacktape docs — if it is not a registered component it will render as raw text. Verify it is available or replace with a numbered list.

issues.statement: All code examples use `CustomResource` as the class name (e.g., `new CustomResource({...})`).
issues.reason: The actual exported class names are `CustomResourceDefinition` and `CustomResourceInstance`, not `CustomResource`. The source class-config.ts defines `className: 'CustomResourceDefinition'` (type `custom-resource-definition`) and `className: 'CustomResourceInstance'` (type `custom-resource-instance`). There is no class named just `CustomResource`. Every CodeBlock import and instantiation on the page uses the wrong class name.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace all `CustomResource` class usages with the correct class names. Definition examples should use `import { CustomResourceDefinition, ... } from 'stacktape'` and `new CustomResourceDefinition({...})`. Add at least one code example showing `CustomResourceInstance` usage: `new CustomResourceInstance({ definitionName: 'webhookProvider', resourceProperties: { callbackUrl: '...' } })`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft has strong structural foundations — correct H2 hierarchy, valid cross-links, accurate timeout/memory values, complete packaging union coverage, and a well-structured FAQ. However, there is one critical issue: every code example uses `CustomResource` as the class name, but the actual exported classes are `CustomResourceDefinition` and `CustomResourceInstance` (confirmed in class-config.ts). This means all CodeBlocks will fail type-checking. Additionally, the page never shows how to create a `CustomResourceInstance` in code, despite the draft's own text explaining that definitions alone don't provision anything. The handler format in the custom-artifact example uses dot notation (`index.handler`) when the source documents colon notation (`filepath:functionName`, e.g., `my-lambda/index.js:default`). The API Reference only covers `CustomResourceDefinitionProps` but not `CustomResourceInstanceProps`, which is a separate peer resource type, not a sub-type.

The draft is broadly aligned with the provided custom-resource type definitions, but it overstates behavior in a few places and has one likely configuration gap: examples define only the custom resource definition, while the source says a definition must be paired with a custom-resource-instance to be used.

issues.statement: The Basic example says the config “defines a custom resource that registers a webhook with an external service during deployment,” but the example only creates a `CustomResource` definition and does not show a custom-resource-instance using it.
issues.reason: The source distinguishes the Lambda-backed definition from an instance and says the definition must be paired with a custom-resource-instance to use. As written, readers may copy a provider definition and expect lifecycle provisioning to run without an instance.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the example/prose so it explicitly says the config defines the backing provider only, or add a grounded custom-resource-instance example that references the definition and passes `resourceProperties`.

issues.statement: The draft says “Stacktape automatically grants IAM permissions and injects environment variables with connection details” for `connectTo` on custom resources, without mentioning the source-supported network-access behavior.
issues.reason: `CustomResourceDefinitionProps` extends `ResourceAccessProps`, whose `connectTo` behavior includes IAM permissions, security group/network access for databases and Redis, and environment variables. The draft’s narrower wording is not wrong for IAM-backed resources, but it is incomplete for VPC-protected resources and may under-explain why `connectTo` matters for databases/Redis.
issues.suggestedFix: State the full source-grounded behavior: `connectTo` grants IAM permissions where relevant, opens network access for resources such as databases and Redis, and injects `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` environment variables.

issues.statement: The draft says `iamRoleStatements` should be used for “AWS services like SES, Rekognition, or Bedrock that aren't managed as Stacktape resources.”
issues.reason: The source explicitly documents `aws:ses` as a `connectTo` macro and describes `iamRoleStatements` examples as Rekognition, Textract, and Bedrock. Calling SES not covered by `connectTo` contradicts the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove SES from the `iamRoleStatements` examples or mention that SES can be handled through `connectTo: ['aws:ses']` if that syntax is documented elsewhere on the page. Keep Rekognition/Textract/Bedrock as raw IAM examples.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly says custom resources provide “automatic rollback support” and that “If an update fails, the previous version is restored.”
issues.reason: Rollback behavior is CloudFormation general behavior, but the provided Stacktape source only states that the Lambda runs on create, update, and delete events. The wording is likely generally true, but it is stronger than the Stacktape-specific source proves.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to CloudFormation lifecycle behavior: custom resource handlers receive create/update/delete events as part of stack operations, including delete events during rollback. Avoid promising exact restoration semantics unless supported by a CloudFormation source.

issues.statement: The draft says custom resource outputs can be used elsewhere through `Fn::GetAtt` via overrides and that custom resources do not expose Stacktape referenceable parameters.
issues.reason: The provided source defines custom resource instance properties but does not include custom resource referenceable parameters in `StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam`, which supports the “no referenceable parameters” part. However, the source does not document a supported Stacktape pattern for using `Fn::GetAtt` against custom resource response `Data`.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the “no Stacktape referenceable parameters” claim, but phrase the `Fn::GetAtt` path cautiously as a CloudFormation escape-hatch pattern rather than a documented Stacktape custom-resource feature.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Realistic, copyable code examples pair Stacktape config with a complete Lambda handler — the first-time reader can go from config to deployed handler without leaving the page.

The 'When NOT to use' section includes a comparison table (custom resource vs. deployment script vs. raw CloudFormation vs. CDK construct) that immediately helps readers pick the right tool without AWS expertise.

The 'Writing the handler' section is properly structured with an event-fields table, an explicit JSON response format block, and a best-practices list — covers the hardest part of custom resources clearly.
The `<Warning>` callout about the mandatory CloudFormation response protocol is well-placed immediately after the handler example, where a first-time reader is most likely to miss it.

All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with exactly `label: 'TypeScript'` tabs — no YAML violations, no fenced TypeScript blocks, no StacktapeConfig import pattern.
Both Lambda packaging modes (buildpack and custom artifact) are covered with working examples and per-variant tradeoffs, satisfying the union-type coverage requirement.
FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, AWS service mechanics, and choosing/comparison questions — and reaches exactly 10 entries.
problems: The `aws:ses` sentence at the end of the 'Connecting to resources' opening paragraph ('The `aws:ses` macro grants full SES email sending permissions when listed in `connectTo`.') is completely disconnected from the surrounding `configBucket` code example and has no code context — it reads as a stray fact appended to unrelated prose.

The `FlowDiagram` component (`<FlowDiagram steps={[...]} />`) is not listed in any MDX component palette documented in the style guide or instructions. Its availability and rendered output are uncertain; if it is unavailable or renders incorrectly the 'How it works' section loses its primary content.

Class names `CustomResourceDefinition` and `CustomResourceInstance` are used throughout all code examples, but the enumerated valid Stacktape class name list provided to writers only includes `CustomResource` — not `CustomResourceDefinition` or `CustomResourceInstance`. If the npm package does not export those exact names, every code block in the page fails the type-checking validator.
mandatoryFixes: Verify that `CustomResourceDefinition` and `CustomResourceInstance` are the actual class names exported by the `stacktape` npm package. The writer's valid-class-name reference list only shows `CustomResource`. If the correct names differ (e.g., both map to a single `CustomResource` class, or are exported differently), update every `new CustomResourceDefinition({...})` and `new CustomResourceInstance({...})` occurrence in all `<CodeBlock intellisense>` tabs to the correct class names.
optionalImprovements: Move the `aws:ses` mention out of the dangling prose sentence and into either a bullet in the connectTo description, a brief inline comment in a code example, or a FAQ answer — anywhere that gives it code or example context rather than floating free after a Bucket-focused paragraph.

Verify `FlowDiagram` component availability. If the component is not part of the production MDX palette, replace the flow steps with a numbered ordered list or a markdown table (e.g., 'Step | Description') so the 'How it works' section still communicates the deploy lifecycle without depending on a possibly unavailable component.

The `<ApiReference definitionName="CustomResourceDefinitionProps" />` covers the definition type only. `CustomResourceInstanceProps` is a separate top-level resource, not a nested sub-type — its two properties (`definitionName`, `resourceProperties`) are documented in the 'Instances' section prose table, which is fine, but adding a note in 'API Reference' pointing readers to the schema entry for `CustomResourceInstanceProps` would make the page self-contained for property lookup.

The `Runtime` section (four sentences, no code example) is thin relative to the other configuration sections, each of which has a working example. Consider adding a three-line `CustomArtifactLambdaPackaging` snippet that sets `runtime: 'nodejs22.x'` explicitly to anchor the concept — especially useful since the custom-artifact mode is when runtime must be set manually.

In the 'Basic example', the `$Secret(external-api-key)` directive reference could include a one-sentence aside noting that secrets must exist in Secrets Manager before deploy (via `stacktape secret:create`) — a common first-run failure for developers new to Stacktape.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The two-part model (definition + instance) is explained clearly and early, which is the most important concept on this page.
The webhook example is realistic, copyable, and includes a complete working Lambda handler — not a toy snippet.

The 'When NOT to use' section names three concrete alternatives (deployment script, raw CloudFormation, CDK constructs) and includes a comparison table, which is exactly what the decision-making audience needs.
The handler writing guide with the event field table and response format JSON block is actionable and correctly framed as part of the CloudFormation protocol, not Stacktape-specific.
10-question FAQ with a genuine category mix — Stacktape-specific, AWS-specific, and comparison questions.
Both Lambda packaging modes (StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging and CustomArtifactLambdaPackaging) are covered with per-variant examples and tradeoff guidance.
The 'Outputs' section is honest about the limitation — custom resources are not in the referenceable params list — instead of silently omitting the topic.
Timeout default (10 s) and max (900 s) are correctly sourced from the type definitions and consistently applied across all examples.
problems: '<FlowDiagram>' is not a documented MDX component in the Stacktape docs style guide or component palette. If it is not registered in the docs renderer, it will produce an empty block in production, silently removing the lifecycle visualization from the 'How it works' section.

The claim 'Per CloudFormation limits, the maximum response payload is 4,096 bytes' is stated as a definitive fact under 'Handler best practices' but does not appear in any of the provided source files. It is an AWS-side limit that should be framed as such or removed.

The `aws:ses` sentence ('The `aws:ses` macro grants full SES email sending permissions when listed in `connectTo`.') appears at the end of a bucket-focused code example in the 'Connecting to resources' section with no contextual bridge. It reads as a dangling fact that was pasted in and not integrated into the surrounding prose.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or replace `<FlowDiagram steps={[...]} />` with an equivalent prose list or a markdown table. `FlowDiagram` is not in the documented Stacktape MDX component palette (style guide lists CodeBlock, ApiReference, ReferenceableParams, Info, Warning, Tip). Using an undocumented component is a mandatory fix per the MDX palette violation rule — penalizing clarity by at least 1 point.

Qualify the 4,096-byte response payload limit. The source files do not document this number. Replace 'Per CloudFormation limits, the maximum response payload is 4,096 bytes' with phrasing that frames it as an AWS CloudFormation fact (e.g., 'AWS CloudFormation imposes a 4,096-byte limit on the response body') or remove it. Stating an unsourced numeric limit as a Stacktape-side rule is a source-grounding violation.
optionalImprovements: Integrate the `aws:ses` mention more naturally. Either add a one-sentence lead-in ('Use service macros like `aws:ses` in `connectTo` for cross-cutting AWS service permissions:') or move it to a dedicated bullet under the connectTo explanation rather than appending it after a bucket-specific code example.

The 'How it works' section describes the two-part model in prose that substantially overlaps with the introduction paragraph. Now that the FlowDiagram will be replaced with prose, consider trimming the section to a short lifecycle list rather than re-explaining the definition/instance split a third time.

Consider mentioning the `overrides` property (present on both `CustomResourceDefinition` and `CustomResourceInstance` in the source types) in a brief note under the API Reference or as an advanced callout. Teams doing cross-region or multi-account setups often need it; a one-liner pointing to the overrides escape-hatch page would serve them.

The response format JSON block could note that `NoEcho` is a valid optional field for masking sensitive output values in CloudFormation — this is a real-world gotcha when the Data object contains tokens or keys.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Complete, copy-paste-ready handler example that handles all three RequestType branches (Create, Delete) with full CloudFormation response protocol — warning box for the response requirement is well-placed.
Comparison table in 'When NOT to use' is concrete and actionable: rows for runtime behavior, lifecycle awareness, and required protocol make the decision easy.
Consistent property-value anchoring throughout every code example — timeout default (10 s), memory/CPU scaling (1,769 MB = 1 vCPU), handler filepath:functionName format are all explained inline.
Event fields table is comprehensive and well-formatted; readers can find what they need without hunting through CloudFormation docs.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo, multiple instances), AWS service use-case (pricing, rollback, response timeout), and comparison (custom resource vs deployment script vs CDK).
Both Lambda packaging modes (stacktape-lambda-buildpack, custom-artifact) are covered at equivalent depth with dedicated code examples, decision guidance, and tradeoffs.
Handler best practices section addresses the three critical gotchas: always-respond, stable PhysicalResourceId on Update (replacement semantics explained), and idempotent Delete for rollback safety.
connectTo example is realistic — uses a named Bucket resource and states the exact env-var names STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_NAME and STP_CONFIG_BUCKET_ARN, matching the STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] pattern.
Outputs limitation section is honest and specific: explains that $ResourceParam() cannot reference custom resource outputs and points to overrides as the escape hatch, without over-complicating it.
ApiReference placement at the bottom is conventional and appropriate for this tutorial-style page; definitionName='CustomResourceDefinitionProps' matches the source type exactly.
problems: The page uses `<FlowDiagram steps={[...]} />` in the 'How it works' section. This component is not listed in the Stacktape MDX component palette (style guide lists CodeBlock, Info, Warning, ApiReference, ReferenceableParams, FeatureComparisonTable). If it does not exist in the rendering system the entire 'How it works' section will either error or silently disappear, leaving four orphaned paragraphs with no visual anchor.

The webhook Lambda handler example omits an Update branch in the switch statement — only Create and Delete are handled. This is the most common source of bugs in real custom resources (callers forget Update semantics). A reader copying this will ship a handler that silently no-ops on all Update events, which is dangerous if the backing resource needs to change during redeployments.

The '4,096 bytes' maximum response payload limit is stated as 'Per CloudFormation limits' but does not appear in any provided source file. It may be correct as an AWS fact, but it reads as a Stacktape-sourced assertion. Callers may rely on this as authoritative.

The 'Basic example' H2 section opener starts with 'This example defines a custom resource definition...' — the pronoun 'This' has no named antecedent in the sentence, reducing extractability. AI engines extracting this paragraph in isolation lose context about what 'this example' refers to.

The 'Runtime' H2 section is a single short paragraph with no code example and no decision signal. It names two packaging modes as the only practical reason to set the property but gives no concrete guidance on which runtimes are valid strings beyond two examples, nor why one would choose a specific version.
mandatoryFixes: Verify that `<FlowDiagram>` is a real, renderable component in the Stacktape docs MDX runtime. If it is not, replace the section with a numbered list or prose description — the surrounding 'How it works' text already explains the four-step flow adequately without the component. Leaving an unknown component in place risks a broken page section on publish.
optionalImprovements: Add an Update branch to the webhook Lambda handler in the basic example — even a minimal 'call PATCH endpoint with the new callbackUrl' skeleton plus a comment that the implementation depends on whether the API supports in-place updates vs delete-and-recreate. Update semantics are the primary footgun for new custom resource authors.

Rewrite the 'Basic example' section opener to name the concept explicitly: e.g., 'The following config defines a webhook provider custom resource — one `CustomResourceDefinition` that backs the Lambda and one `CustomResourceInstance` that triggers it during stack operations.' This makes the paragraph self-contained for citation engines.

Attribute the 4,096-byte response payload claim explicitly as an AWS CloudFormation constraint with a link to the CloudFormation custom resources documentation, rather than as a bare assertion. This protects the page's factual credibility if the limit changes.

Expand the 'Runtime' section with a one-line table of runtime strings grouped by language (nodejs22.x, nodejs20.x; python3.12, python3.11; java17, java11; etc.) drawn from the Lambda packaging docs. The current single paragraph adds little value without concrete runtime values or a link to the runtime list.

The `iamRoleStatements` code example in 'Connecting to resources' has `timeout: 30` which, like all other examples, is explained as non-default — but the explanation says 'This gives you fine-grained control...' about `iamRoleStatements` without briefly noting the timeout change. A one-liner consistent with the other examples ('timeout is 30 s here — default is 10') would keep the pattern uniform.

Consider adding a `<ReferenceableParams resource="custom-resource-definition" />` callout after the API Reference if the resource exposes any referenceable parameters — or explicitly state that it exposes none, which would be consistent with the Outputs section's existing claim about $ResourceParam() not working for custom resources.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded in the source material and accurately represents the Custom Resource Definition and Instance types. Property names, defaults, constraints, and cross-links all check out against the provided source files. The two-part model (definition + instance), packaging modes, timeout/memory defaults, connectTo behavior, and referenceable parameters claim are all correctly sourced. The FAQ answers are accurate and well-balanced between Stacktape-specific and general AWS knowledge. Minor issues relate to documentation-quality completeness rather than factual errors.

issues.statement: The 'Packaging' section names both modes and gives each a code example, but the 'Stacktape Lambda buildpack' subsection lacks decision-support depth — it says 'This is the recommended choice for most custom resources' but provides no tradeoff signal (cost, image size, build time) or concrete scenario beyond 'point it at your handler file'. The 'Custom artifact' subsection is slightly better but also thin.
issues.reason: Per documentation-quality discipline, each opt-in / variant section should have: opening paragraph + working example + 2-4 sentences of tradeoff/depth. The buildpack subsection has an opening sentence, a code example, and one sentence restating the timeout. No tradeoff between the two modes beyond 'use this unless you have a pre-existing build pipeline'. Reader cannot decide between modes based on this.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 1-2 sentences to each packaging subsection comparing the two: buildpack handles bundling and dependency resolution automatically but limits control; custom artifact gives full control but requires maintaining your own build pipeline. Mention that the buildpack supports JS/TS/Python/Java/Go/Ruby/PHP/.NET (per source JSDoc) while custom artifact works with any runtime since you provide the zip.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the custom resource source shape and avoids the major forbidden config-example patterns. The main issues are a few recommendations and examples that go beyond the provided Stacktape source, plus a copyable handler that does not actually handle Update events even though the page repeatedly frames custom resources as Create/Update/Delete lifecycle hooks.

issues.statement: The "When to use" section recommends custom resources for "Cross-account AWS resources" and "Custom validation or seed operations."
issues.reason: The provided custom-resource source describes Lambda-backed lifecycle management for external resources not natively supported by Stacktape or CloudFormation. It does not document cross-account provisioning as a Stacktape custom-resource use case, and seed operations are later presented as a deployment-script use case. This could lead readers to choose custom resources for one-off tasks rather than lifecycle-managed external resources.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the guidance to lifecycle-managed external resources, third-party APIs, SaaS services, and custom infrastructure. Move seeding/validation toward deployment scripts unless the operation creates, updates, and deletes external lifecycle state. Remove the cross-account example unless another provided source explicitly supports it.

evidence.quote:
Your Lambda function runs on stack create, update, and delete events to manage external resources
 * (third-party APIs, SaaS services, custom infrastructure). Pair with `custom-resource-instance` to use.

issues.statement: The basic webhook Lambda handler is presented as managing stack operations, but the code only handles `Create` and `Delete`; `Update` silently succeeds without updating the external webhook.
issues.reason: The source says custom resource Lambdas run on create, update, and delete events. A copyable handler that no-ops `Update` conflicts with the lifecycle framing and can mislead readers into shipping a handler that ignores `resourceProperties` changes.
issues.suggestedFix: Add an `Update` case that updates or replaces the external webhook, or explicitly state that the example treats updates as a no-op and should be extended for mutable `resourceProperties`.

evidence.quote: Your Lambda function runs on stack create, update, and delete events to manage external resources

issues.statement: The Runtime section gives concrete runtime examples: `nodejs22.x`, `python3.12`, and `java17`.
issues.reason: The provided source only exposes `runtime?: LambdaRuntime` and says the runtime is auto-detected from the file extension if unspecified. It does not enumerate specific supported runtime literals, and runtime values are concrete config inputs.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the concrete runtime literals, or add the source that defines `LambdaRuntime`. Safer wording: "Set `runtime` to a supported Lambda runtime when you need to pin the version."

issues.statement: The draft uses `<FlowDiagram ... />`.
issues.reason: The prompt does not include an available MDX component list that confirms `FlowDiagram` exists. Without component-registry evidence, this is only a component-sanity concern, not a factual source issue.
issues.suggestedFix: Confirm `FlowDiagram` is an allowed docs component, or replace it with a short ordered list using standard Markdown.

positiveFindings: The Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and do not hand-author YAML.
The draft correctly documents the definition/instance split using `CustomResourceDefinitionProps` and `CustomResourceInstanceProps` from the source.
The timeout default and maximum, memory range, and CPU scaling statement match the provided `custom-resources.d.ts` source.
The `connectTo` bucket environment variables and permissions are consistent with the `ResourceAccessProps` source for `Bucket`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the primary `CustomResourceDefinitionProps` surface well: packaging, environment, runtime, timeout, memory, `connectTo`, and `iamRoleStatements` are all mentioned, and the page includes exactly one API reference for the primary Props type. I found no API-completeness, union-variant coverage, or demonstrativeness issues that meet the medium/high threshold for this auditor role.
positiveFindings: The page documents every important property on `CustomResourceDefinitionProps`: `packaging`, `environment`, `runtime`, `timeout`, `memory`, plus inherited `connectTo` and `iamRoleStatements`.
The Lambda packaging union is acknowledged with both supported variants for this source set: `stacktape-lambda-buildpack` and `custom-artifact`.
The `CustomResourceInstanceProps` shape is covered with `definitionName` and `resourceProperties`, even though the page primary type is `CustomResourceDefinitionProps`.
Each `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example demonstrates the section topic: basic definition plus instance, multiple instances, both packaging modes, `connectTo`, and raw IAM statements.
The API reference uses the exact primary metadata type name: `<ApiReference definitionName="CustomResourceDefinitionProps" />`.
